text: Robotic end-block radical nephrectomy and retrocaval lymphadenectomy. An eighty-seven-year-old patient, having a surgical history, two cesarean sections and abdominal eventration, presented with an eleven-centimeter right renal mass with lymph node involvement, in which a seven-centimeter compressive retrocaval adenopathy, classified CT-TRA, N-II, M-Zero.

The surgeon accesses the surgical field transperitoneally, following the detachment of the right colic. The inferior vena cava is dissected alongside the genital vein, providing access to the anastomosis of the contralateral left renal vein. The liver is mobilized, and hepatic veins are divided to enhance exposure.

The surgeon progressively dissects the left renal vein on all sides and secures it with a loop. The renal artery is dissected and clamped through an interaortical caval approach on both sides of the left renal vein.

The surgeon completes the dissection and division of the right renal vein, exposing a large adenopathy. The retrocaval lymph nodes are carefully detached in a step-by-step manner, starting from the anterior face, using a retro- and interaorticocaval approach.

The surgeon performs meticulous lymphostasis using hemostatic clips. The ureter is identified and divided. The kidney is detached at its inferior pole, along with the surrounding perirenal fat and Gerota's fascia.
The surgeon performs an adrenalectomy prior to detaching the kidney at its superior pole. Subsequently, a non-block intrafacial resection of the kidney and associated ganglion is carried out.

The excised tissues are extracted in a surgical bag through a pre-existing sub-umbilical midline scar. The surgical field shows no signs of post-operative complications, with pre-operative bleeding estimated at 250 cc.

Pathology reported a type II tubulopapillary renal cell carcinoma from an grade IV with massive lymph nodes invasion. However, the resection was margin-free and the tumor finally classified Pititra N-II R-Zero C-M-Zero. CT scan follow-up at two and eight postoperative months didn't show any sign of disease recurrence.
Minimally invasive surgery is technically feasible for locally advanced renal tumors with massive lymph node invasions with a low per- and postoperative morbidity.
